spells.name: Cinder-Scale Barrier
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — fire element)
spells.description: You sweep your bloodline sigil through the air, and a curtain of spinning draconic scales forged from molten cinder erupts into existence. The wall appears in a straight line up to 100 feet long, 20 feet high, and 5 feet thick, or a ringed wall up to 60 feet in diameter. When a creature enters or starts its turn in the wall's space, it must make a Dexterity saving throw. On a failed save, it takes 6d10 fire damage as the superheated scales tear through flesh; on a successful save, it takes half damage. If the wall appears in a creature's space, that creature makes the save immediately. The wall provides three-quarters cover. Flammable objects within 5 feet of the wall's path catch fire instantly — the scales shed embers that hunger for kindling. The barrier's heat is visible as a shimmering orange haze, and the grinding of the scales produces a sound like a thousand swords being sharpened at once.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 7th level or higher, the damage increases by 1d10 for each slot level above 6th.

spells.name: Storm-Blood Cascade
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — storm element)
spells.description: Your bloodline pulses with the fury of a storm dragon, and a bolt of blue-white lightning erupts from your outstretched hand toward a creature you can see. The target must make a Dexterity saving throw. On a failure, it takes 10d8 lightning damage as the draconic current sears through its veins, leaving branching scars like lightning-struck glass. On a success, it takes half. The lightning then arcs to up to three additional creatures of your choice within 30 feet of the first, each of which must also make the save, suffering the same fate. The cascade bears the unmistakable crack of the Old Tongue — any creature familiar with draconic lore recognizes the syllables of the Storm-Wyrm bloodline embedded in the thunder.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 7th level or higher, one additional bolt leaps from each target to a new creature for each slot level above 6th.

spells.name: Void-Blood Siphon
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — void element)
spells.description: You utter syllables of the Void-Wyrm bloodline, and a sphere of absolute darkness blossoms from a point you choose within range before collapsing inward with a soundless shriek. Each creature in a 60-foot-radius sphere must make a Constitution saving throw. A creature takes 8d6 necrotic damage on a failed save as the void hungrily drinks its life force, the warmth draining from its body as frost forms on its eyelids; on a successful save, it takes half. A creature killed by this spell leaves no mark on its flesh — it simply stops. The soul is not consumed, but witnesses swear the body looks... emptied. Plants in the area wither and blacken. The spell leaves a lingering chill in the air for 1 minute, during which time any healing received in the area is halved.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 7th level or higher, the damage increases by 2d6 for each slot level above 6th.

spells.name: Forethought Ward
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — any element, and an item of personal significance to the caster)
spells.description: You inscribe a contingency sigil upon your own bloodline, tying a protective or reactive spell to a trigger condition you specify. Choose a spell of 5th level or lower that you can cast, that has a casting time of 1 action, and that can target you. Describe a circumstance — "when I fall below half health," "when I am struck by silver," "if an Inquisitor enters my presence" — and the ward will cast that spell on you instantly when the condition is met. The ward glows faintly beneath your skin, visible only to those who can perceive aether. You may have only one Forethought Ward active at a time. The spell woven into the ward is cast at its base level and consumes its own Aether-Core stored during the ritual. The ancients called this the Dragon's Foresight — a sliver of the predictive instinct possessed by the Great Wyrms.

spells.name: Rise Dredged
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — void or flesh element, and three intact corpses)
spells.description: You speak the forbidden words of the Flesh-Wyrm bloodline over three corpses, channeling your aether into the dead flesh until it twitches, then rises. Up to three Medium or Small humanoid corpses within range animate as dread dredged — the Solis-Grave equivalent of ghouls, their flesh pale and slick as if pulled from the Sump canals. They obey your verbal commands for 24 hours, after which their false life expires and the corpses collapse into black sludge. The dredged retain a fragment of the caster's bloodline sigil burned into their foreheads — a mark that an Inquisitor would recognize instantly. Maintaining control requires a bonus action each round to issue a command; without one, they defend themselves against the nearest threat. You may assert or reassert control over up to three dredged created by this casting; existing dredged beyond this limit are released. This spell is punishable by Pyre execution in all six Sovereign Houses.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 7th level, you create four dread dredged. At 8th: five. At 9th: six.

spells.name: Wyrm's Unmaking
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — void element)
spells.description: You trace the Unmaking Sigil — the only bloodline crest shared by every dragon bloodline, said to be the mark the First Wyrm used to erase its mistakes from creation — and a thin beam of pale green light springs from your finger to a target you can see. The target must make a Dexterity saving throw. On a failed save, it takes 10d6 + 40 force damage as the Old Tongue commands its form to forget itself. If this damage reduces the target to 0 hit points, it is entirely unmade: its body, its possessions, and any nonmagical objects it carries are reduced to fine gray ash. Nothing remains but a faint scorch mark and the smell of ozone. A creature unmade by this spell can only be restored to life by a Rebirth of the Bloodline spell or an equivalent 7th-level or greater restoration. This spell disintegrates nonmagical matter instantly and can destroy a 10-foot cube of any nonmagical object. Magical objects are unaffected. The beam is silent — witnesses describe a moment of absolute stillness before the target simply ceases to be.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 7th level or higher, the damage increases by 3d6 for each slot level above 6th.

spells.name: Gaze of the Dragon-Lord
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — any element matching your bloodline)
spells.description: Your eyes ignite with the terrible authority of a sovereign wyrm, glowing with your bloodline's elemental hue. For the duration, your gaze becomes a weapon. As an action on each of your turns, you can fix your stare upon a creature within 60 feet and invoke one of three aspects: the Sleepless Terror (target must succeed on a Wisdom save or be frightened of you until the spell ends), the Sickness Unto Death (target must succeed on a Constitution save or be poisoned and take 4d8 psychic damage at the start of each of its turns until it saves), or the Wyrm's Slumber (target must succeed on a Wisdom save or fall unconscious until damaged, the spell ends, or an action is used to shake it awake). A creature that successfully saves against your gaze is immune to that aspect for 24 hours. The gaze costs no action to maintain — your eyes simply burn until the spell ends. Those who meet your stare swear they see the outline of wings behind you.

spells.name: Call the Wild-Blood
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — flesh element)
spells.description: You speak the Old Tongue into the earth and call a Wild-Blood spirit from the spaces between — a primal draconic entity born of the dragons who became forests, rivers, and mountains. A fey-like creature of challenge rating 6 or lower manifests in an unoccupied space you can see. The spirit is friendly to you and your companions for the duration, acting on its own turn. It obeys verbal commands (no action required), but if you give none, it defends itself. The spirit disappears when it drops to 0 hit points or the spell ends. The entity appears as a creature woven from elemental dragon essence — a stag with scales of bark, a serpent of living water, a wolf whose fur is autumn leaves — and it speaks Old Tongue. The Inquisition considers this spell a form of consorting with unsanctioned powers; if witnessed, the caster is branded a heretic.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 7th level or higher, the challenge rating increases by 1 for each slot level above 6th.

spells.name: Basilisk's Gaze
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — flesh element)
spells.description: Your bloodline sigil twists into the pattern of the Stone-Wyrm — a creature so ancient its very gaze calcifies the living. A creature you can see within range must make a Constitution saving throw, its limbs stiffening as gray scales of stone creep across its skin. On a failure, it is restrained as its flesh petrifies. It must repeat the save at the end of each of its turns. If it fails three times (not necessarily consecutive), it is permanently turned to stone and subjected to the petrified condition. If it succeeds three times, the spell ends. Successes and failures need not be consecutive; track both until the target collects three of a kind. A creature turned to stone by this spell is not dead — it exists in a state of suspended animation, its soul trapped within the stone. A Greater Restoration, Scale-Mend, or similar 6th-level or greater restorative magic can reverse the effect. The stone bears swirling patterns matching the caster's bloodline sigil. Statues left by this spell are known in the Citadel as Basilisk-Gifts and are considered sacred — they cannot be destroyed without a Sovereign's pardon.

spells.name: Sanctum of the Bloodline
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — matching your bloodline element, plus Aether-Cores worth at least 1,000 GC total which are consumed by the ritual)
spells.description: You consecrate a 40,000-square-foot area (up to 200 feet on a side) by walking its perimeter and marking the boundary with your bloodline sigil. The sanctum wards against specific creature types you designate — celestials, elementals, fey, fiends, or any creature with a bloodline purity of 50% or higher. Chosen creatures cannot enter the area willingly, cannot teleport into it, and take 5d10 radiant damage if they attempt to breach the ward. Additionally, you may key the sanctum to a password spoken in the Old Tongue, and you may declare a type of damage (chosen from acid, cold, fire, lightning, necrotic, or poison) to which all creatures within the sanctum gain resistance. The ward is visible as a shimmering draconic script that hovers at the boundary — readable by any who know the Old Tongue. This spell is used by noble Houses to protect their estates and by Ordained to shield cathedrals. The Inquisition uses Forbiddance to seal buildings suspected of harboring unsanctioned bloodlines.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 7th level or higher, the damage increases by 1d10 and the duration increases to 10 days (7th), 30 days (8th), or until dispelled (9th).

spells.name: Aegis of the Sovereign
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — any element)
spells.description: You raise your bloodline sigil as a shimmering barrier extends from you in a 10-foot-radius sphere. For the duration, the sphere moves with you and remains centered on you. Any spell of 5th level or lower cast from outside the sphere cannot affect creatures or objects within it — the magic hits the barrier and unravels, its draconic syllables silenced. Even spells cast using higher-level slots are blocked if the base spell level is 5th or lower. The barrier itself is a translucent sheath in the color of your bloodline's element, rippling like heat haze. Spells of 6th level or higher penetrate the barrier normally. You and your allies may leave and re-enter freely. The Aegis does not block spells cast from within the sphere targeting creatures outside, nor does it block breath weapons, aether-fueled devices, or spell-like abilities that do not use a spell slot. This is the most coveted defensive spell among nobles who fear assassination by rival bloodlines.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 7th level or higher, the barrier blocks spells of one level higher for each slot level above 6th.

spells.name: Dragon's Rebuke
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — void or fire element)
spells.description: You speak a single syllable of the Old Tongue — the word the First Wyrm used to punish its disobedient children — and a wave of withering, dragon-borne illness crashes into a creature you can see. The target must make a Constitution saving throw. On a failure, it takes 14d6 necrotic damage as its blood runs black and its veins glow with sickly aetheric light. On a success, it takes half. The spell cannot reduce the target's hit points below 1. The target's flesh blisters with scale-shaped welts that persist for 1d4 days and mark the victim as having been touched by sovereign wrath. This spell is favored by Ordained as a tool of chastisement — it wounds without killing, leaving the victim alive to contemplate their transgression. Among the Houses, casting Dragon's Rebuke on a member of a rival bloodline is considered a formal declaration of enmity.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 7th level or higher, the damage increases by 2d6 for each slot level above 6th.

spells.name: Vitalis Surge
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — flesh or fire element)
spells.description: You call upon the Life-Wyrm — the primordial dragon whose blood became the first heartbeat. A wave of golden draconic light washes over a creature you can see, mending flesh and purging affliction. The target regains 70 hit points and is cured of blindness, deafness, and any diseases. This spell has no effect on constructs or undead. The healing manifests as a brief vision: the recipient sees the silhouette of a golden dragon unfurling its wings behind their own shadow. Survivors of near-fatal wounds describe the sensation as "waking from a fever into a dragon's embrace." In Solis-Grave, this spell is the highest form of battlefield medicine — those who can cast it are valued above gold in any mercenary company or noble household.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 7th level or higher, the healing increases by 10 for each slot level above 6th.

spells.name: Bloodline Communion
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — fire element, and a feast worth at least 500 GC which is consumed)
spells.description: You prepare a ritual feast infused with aether drawn from a Pristine Aether-Core, and up to twelve creatures partake. The food appears as if from nowhere, arriving on golden platters wreathed in the faint glow of dragon-fire. Those who consume the feast gain 2d10 temporary hit points, become immune to poison and the frightened condition, and gain advantage on Wisdom saving throws for 24 hours. Their maximum hit points also increase by 2d10 for the same duration. The feast evokes a shared vision among the diners: they briefly see the great conclave of the First Wyrms, gathered in a hall of obsidian and starlight, and feel the warmth of belonging to a bloodline older than the world. This is the traditional spell cast before a Sovereign-led military campaign or a noble wedding. The Inquisition tolerates the practice — barely — because several bloodline saints were canonized during Bloodline Communions.

spells.name: Wyrm's Beguiling Rite
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — any element)
spells.description: You begin the Beguiling Rite — an ancient draconic courting dance that no mortal mind can witness without succumbing. A creature you can see within range must make a Wisdom saving throw. On a failure, it begins a comic, involuntary dance: shuffling, capering, spinning in place for the duration. Its movement speed is halved, it has disadvantage on Dexterity saving throws and attack rolls, and other creatures have advantage on attack rolls against it. As an action, the dancer can attempt a Wisdom saving throw to end the effect early. There is no sound to the rite — the target dances in silence, compelled by the geometry of your sigil alone. Bystanders report feeling an urge to tap their feet. The spell was first developed by a Lore-Keeper who witnessed the mating display of a silver dragon and spent a decade translating it into a spell. It has since become a signature of Solis-Grave bards, used as often in courtly intrigue as in battle.

spells.name: Vessel of the Usurper
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — void element, and a gemstone or reliquary worth at least 500 GC)
spells.description: You enact the Usurpation Rite — the spell that created the first God-Shroud and the sin for which the dragon Ouroborath was exiled from the First Conclave. Your soul leaves your body and enters the material component, which becomes a soul-vessel. From the vessel, you may attempt to possess a humanoid creature within 100 feet (Wisdom save negates). On a failure, your soul enters the host and the host's soul is trapped in the vessel. You control the host body, using its physical statistics but your own mental stats, class features, and spellcasting. You cannot use the host's class features or spellcasting. If the host body dies, your soul returns to the vessel if it is within 100 feet; otherwise, you die. If the vessel is destroyed while your soul is inside it, you die instantly with no chance of resurrection short of a Rebirth of the Bloodline. The Inquisition considers this the most heretical spell in existence — possession of another body without consent of the Dragon-Gods is an unforgivable crime. The Usurpation Rite is suppressed knowledge; grimoires containing it are burned on sight.

spells.name: Sovereign's Decree
spells.components: V, M (Pristine Aether-Core — fire element)
spells.description: You speak words in the Old Tongue that resonate with the authority of a ruling wyrm, and up to twelve creatures of your choice within range must make a Wisdom saving throw. On a failure, a creature is charmed by you and follows a course of action you describe in one or two sentences —"defend this gate until relieved," "surrender your weapons and kneel," "the man in blue is an enemy — seize him" — for the duration, provided the suggestion is not obviously self-destructive. The decree must sound reasonable; asking a creature to stab itself, immolate, or betray a sworn blood-oath will automatically end the spell for that creature. A creature that saves is unaware you attempted to influence it. The words carry a physical weight — listeners feel pressure on their chest, as if a dragon's claw rests there. This is the spell of rulers, not warriors. It has ended more blood feuds than any blade.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 7th level or higher, the duration increases to 10 days (7th), 30 days (8th), or 1 year and 1 day (9th).

spells.name: Solar Wyrm's Radiance
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — fire element)
spells.description: You hold your bloodline sigil before you like a lens, and a blazing beam of golden-white dragon-light — the radiance of the Solar Wyrm, the dragon who ate a star — shines forth in a line 5 feet wide and 60 feet long. Each creature in the line must make a Constitution saving throw, taking 6d8 radiant damage on a failure or half on a success. A creature that fails is also blinded until the end of your next turn. On each of your subsequent turns, you can use your action to sweep the beam in a new direction. Undead and creatures of the Void take double damage from this spell and have disadvantage on the saving throw. The light is considered true sunlight; creatures with sunlight sensitivity react accordingly. The beam burns with the color of your bloodline — golden for fire, silver-white for storm, pale blue for frost — but always carries the unmistakable quality of dragon-light. Lore-Keepers sing that the Solar Wyrm's Radiance was the first spell ever cast: the moment the First Wyrm used its own light to create the sun.

spells.name: Dragon's Third Eye
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — any element, and ointment made from dragon-scale powder)
spells.description: You anoint a willing creature's brow with dragon-scale ointment, tracing the sigil of the All-Seeing Wyrm — a dragon so ancient it grew a third eye to watch over its entire bloodline simultaneously. For the duration, the creature has truesight out to 120 feet, noticing secret doors hidden by magic, seeing into the Ethereal Plane, and perceiving the true form of shapechangers and creatures under illusion. The creature can also see the aetheric glow around magical objects and effects, and can perceive the bloodline sigil that hovers faintly above any creature with purity 10% or higher. The recipient's eyes split vertically during the spell's duration — a second pupil forms, golden and slit-pupiled. This is painless but deeply unsettling to observers. The effect cannot be hidden without magical disguise. Ordained use this spell to verify bloodline claims during Citadel ceremonies. The Inquisition's most feared agents — the Scale-Eyes — all bear a permanent version of this spell burned into their sight.

spells.name: Rime-Scale Wall
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — frost element)
spells.description: You exhale a long breath that freezes into a wall of interlocking draconic scales made of solid ice. The wall appears on a solid surface in a line up to 60 feet long, 20 feet high, and 1 foot thick, or a hemispherical dome up to 30 feet in diameter. When the wall appears, each creature within its area must make a Dexterity saving throw, taking 10d6 cold damage on a failure or half on a success. The wall is opaque, providing total cover, with AC 12 and 30 hit points per 10-foot section. A destroyed section leaves a curtain of frigid mist that deals 2d6 cold damage to any creature passing through it for the remaining duration. If a creature is touching the wall when it takes fire damage, the wall absorbs some of the heat — the creature takes an additional 5d6 cold damage as the rime-scales flash-melt and refreeze around its flesh. The wall's surface is etched with the caster's bloodline sigil in frost patterns that reform if scraped away.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 7th level or higher, the damage increases by 2d6 for each slot level above 6th.

spells.name: Call of the Dragon Court
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — fire element, and an offering appropriate to the entity called, worth 500-5,000 GC)
spells.description: You perform the Summoning Rite and beseech the Dragon Court — the celestial parliament of the Dragon-Gods — for aid. An emissary from the Court arrives: a draconic celestial spirit in a form appropriate to the request. You may ask the emissary to perform one task: deliver a message anywhere in the planes, guide you and up to eight companions through a dangerous region for 8 hours, provide knowledge about a specific bloodline or draconic artifact, or fight alongside you in a single battle (treat as a celestial of CR 5 or lower). The emissary's form reflects the nature of the request — a small golden dragon for messages, a hound of silver fire for guidance, a luminous serpent for knowledge, a winged warrior of bronze for battle. The entity is not compelled; if the task is dishonorable or counter to the Dragon-Gods' interests, it may refuse or demand a greater offering. The spell's cost in GC represents the offering; a miserly gift may be rejected outright, while a generous one may earn the emissary's favor and a potential ongoing relationship. Ordained who cast this regularly are considered to have a "seat at the Court" — a mark of the highest clerical prestige.

spells.name: Hearth-Sigil
spells.components: V, M (Pristine Aether-Core — fire element, and a sanctuary prepared with your bloodline sigil)
spells.description: You trace the Hearth-Sigil — the first sigil every bloodline child learns, the mark of home — and you and up to five willing creatures within 5 feet are instantly transported to a sanctuary you have previously consecrated. The sanctuary must be a place strongly linked to your bloodline: your ancestral home, a temple dedicated to your family's dragon patron, or a location where you have spent at least 24 hours performing a binding ritual. The destination must be on the same plane of existence. If no sanctuary exists, the spell fails and the slot is wasted. Teleportation is silent and instantaneous; one moment you are there, the next you smell the incense of your safehold. Each bloodline's Hearth-Sigil is unique and cannot be replicated — it is as personal as a signature. This is the spell every noble parent teaches their children first. In a world of Inquisitors and blood feuds, the ability to go home is worth more than any weapon.

spells.name: Sovereign's Mandate
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — fire element)
spells.description: Your bloodline authority manifests as an overwhelming draconic presence, and you issue a single-word command in the Old Tongue to up to six creatures of your choice within range. Each target must make a Wisdom saving throw. On a failure, a creature is compelled to follow the command on its next turn. The command must be a single word — Kneel, Flee, Halt, Drop, Grovel, Approach — and a creature that cannot hear you or does not share a language with you has advantage on the save. The command is not telepathic; the creatures hear the word spoken in the Old Tongue, and its meaning is felt viscerally regardless of language. A creature ordered to take directly harmful action (such as "Jump" off a cliff) is unaffected, but indirect danger ("Flee" through a trap-filled hall) is permitted. The Sovereign's Mandate is not a request — it is an assertion of blood-right. Creatures with 50% or higher purity have advantage on the save, as their own bloodlines resist subjugation. This spell is a core component of the Rite of Ascension, used to establish a new Sovereign's authority before the assembled Houses.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 7th level or higher, you can affect two additional creatures for each slot level above 6th.

spells.name: Debt of Crimson
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — void or flesh element, and a drop of the target's blood or a personal possession)
spells.description: You invoke the Blood Debt — an ancient pact-law that binds Penitents to their patrons — and turn it upon a creature. One creature you can see must make a Charisma saving throw. On a failure, crimson sigils of debt burn into its forearms, and at the start of each of its turns for the duration, it takes 4d6 psychic damage as its own bloodline rebels against it, the unpaid debt gnawing at its soul. The creature can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on a success. On a successful initial save, the target takes half the initial damage and suffers no ongoing effect. A creature killed by this spell leaves a corpse with its bloodline sigil inverted — a mark of a broken pact that any Penitent or Inquisitor can identify. The Debt of Crimson bypasses resistance to psychic damage because it targets the bloodline rather than the mind. This spell is the signature of Penitents who serve the Void-Wyrm, but it has been adopted by other warlocks whose patrons demand payment.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 7th level or higher, the ongoing damage increases by 1d6 for each slot level above 6th.

spells.name: Leyline Communion
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — any element)
spells.description: You open your bloodline to the draconic ley lines that crisscross the world — the invisible veins of aether left by the First Wyrms as they shaped creation. For the next hour, your purity is treated as 10% higher for the purposes of Spell Safety DC calculations only, and you gain the ability to sense the direction and distance of the nearest Dragon-Touched Location within 1 mile. Additionally, you may ask the ley lines one question about the history of the area you stand in — the resonance of draconic magic that has been cast here, the bloodlines that have passed through, or the presence of any slain dragons whose bones still pulse beneath the earth. The answer arrives as a vision: a flash of images and Old Tongue syllables lasting no more than 6 seconds. The ley lines do not know the future and cannot reveal information deliberately hidden by a creature of greater purity than your own. Druids and Wild-Bloods favor this spell for locating ancient dragon graves and avoiding areas tainted by Inquisitor wards.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 7th level or higher, the purity bonus increases to +15% (7th) or +20% (8th+), and you may ask one additional question per slot level above 6th.

spells.name: Sigil of Concealment
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — any element)
spells.description: You draw your bloodline sigil inward — a painful act of magical suppression — wrapping your draconic heritage in a sheath of null-aether. For the duration, your bloodline is undetectable to Inquisitor wards, Scale-Eye agents, Dragon's Third Eye, and any spell or effect that detects or reveals bloodline purity. Your purity is treated as 0% for the purposes of any detection effect (but NOT for spellcasting — you cast using your actual purity). Additionally, you ignore the -10% purity penalty for Inquisitor proximity while this spell is active. The concealment is not perfect: if you cast a spell of 4th level or higher, the Sigil flickers for 1 round, during which detection is possible. Casting a spell of 6th level or higher immediately ends the concealment. The suppression is uncomfortable — many casters describe it as "holding your breath for hours" — and you have disadvantage on Constitution saving throws to maintain concentration on other spells. This spell is essential for any unsanctioned caster operating within a Citadel-patrolled city.

spells.name: Blade of the Wyrm-King
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — fire or storm element)
spells.description: You conjure a blade-shaped rift of pure draconic aether — the sword-form of Ourobathrax the Wyrm-King, first sovereign of dragonkind — that hovers in an unoccupied space within range. The blade is a shimmering plane of force roughly the size and shape of a greatsword, glowing with the color of your bloodline. When it appears, you may make a melee spell attack against a creature within 5 feet of the blade, dealing 4d12 force damage on a hit. As a bonus action on each of your subsequent turns, you can move the blade up to 20 feet and repeat the attack against a creature within 5 feet of its new position. The blade can also be mentally commanded to attack a creature within range as your action. The sword is semi-sentient and will hum the Old Tongue word for "mercy" when near a creature with lower purity than the caster — a detail that unnerves even experienced casters. The blade passes through nonmagical barriers but cannot harm ethereal creatures.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 8th level or higher, the damage increases by 1d12 for each slot level above 7th.

spells.name: Delayed Cinder Burst
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — fire element)
spells.description: You conjure a bead of compressed draconic fire — a miniature star no larger than a pearl — and place it at a point within range, where it hovers, pulsing with building pressure. The bead glows faintly in colors that shift through your bloodline's spectrum and hums at a frequency just below hearing. At the end of your turn, if the bead has not yet detonated, its damage increases by 1d6. You may use a bonus action on a subsequent turn to detonate it early, or it detonates when the spell ends. When it explodes, each creature in a 20-foot-radius sphere must make a Dexterity saving throw. On a failure, a creature takes fire damage equal to the accumulated total (base 12d6, plus 1d6 per round delayed, maximum 22d6 after 10 rounds). On a success, it takes half. The explosion leaves no smoke — only a brief, blinding flash and a circle of blackened ground. Anything flammable not worn or carried ignites. The bead can be moved up to 30 feet as a bonus action, and it detonates early if touched by a creature. The Delayed Cinder Burst was invented by an Archon Caster who wanted to give enemies time to reconsider their choices.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 8th level or higher, the base damage increases by 1d6 for each slot level above 7th.

spells.name: Dragon-God's Edict
spells.components: V
spells.description: You speak a single utterance of the Old Tongue — a word so potent that lesser dragons go their entire lives without earning the right to hear it. The word erupts from you in a 30-foot-radius pulse of divine draconic authority. Each creature of your choice within range must make a Charisma saving throw. The edict's effect depends on the target's current hit points and its purity relative to your own. Creatures with 20 hit points or fewer are instantly deafened and rendered unconscious for 1 hour (until damaged or shaken awake). Creatures with 30 hit points or fewer that fail are blinded, deafened, and stunned for 1 minute. Creatures with 40 hit points or fewer that fail are deafened and blinded for 1 minute. All other creatures that fail are deafened for 1 minute. Creatures with purity equal to or greater than your own have advantage on the save, and creatures with a purity of 75% or higher are immune to all effects except deafness. The word also instantly kills all nonmagical plants within range and banishes any celestial, elemental, fey, or fiend with fewer than 15 hit points to its home plane. The Inquisition has approved exactly three recorded uses of this spell in Citadel history; each was followed by a century of theological debate.

spells.name: Step Between Scales
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — void or storm element)
spells.description: You perform the Scale-Parting Rite and step sideways through the boundary of the material world into the Ethereal Plane — the space between the scales of the Great Wyrm whose body is the universe. You and up to three willing creatures within 10 feet enter the Border Ethereal, a gray mist-world where solid objects appear as ghostly outlines. In the Border Ethereal, you can move in any direction (including up and down) at half speed, pass through solid objects, and perceive the Material Plane out to 60 feet. Creatures and objects on the Material Plane cannot perceive or interact with you. You cannot affect or be affected by anything on the Material Plane except force damage, abjuration effects, and creatures that also exist on the Ethereal Plane. When the spell ends, you and your companions return to the Material Plane at your current positions, which may be inside solid objects — resulting in 1d10 force damage and shunting to the nearest unoccupied space. The Ethereal Plane in Solis-Grave is known as the Space Between Scales, and it is said that if you travel far enough, you can see the outline of the World-Wyrm's eye watching you.

spells.name: Dragon's Death Mark
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — void element)
spells.description: You point at a creature, and your bloodline sigil appears on its chest as a black brand, burning through armor and flesh alike. The target must make a Constitution saving throw. On a failure, it takes 7d8 + 30 necrotic damage as the Death Mark consumes its life force. On a success, it takes half. A humanoid killed by this spell rises at the start of your next turn as a dread dredged permanently under your control (no action required to command, but you may only control one such dredged at a time). The risen creature retains the black brand on its chest — a permanent mark visible to any who know draconic lore, identifying it as having been slain by a caster of the Death Mark. The brand cannot be removed by any means short of a Rebirth of the Bloodline or Wish. This spell is forbidden by every Sovereign House and the Citadel alike — it is considered a direct violation of the Compact of the Wyrm-Council, which prohibits the enslavement of souls through draconic magic. Casting it is punishable by immediate Pyre execution, no trial permitted.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 8th level or higher, the damage increases by 1d8 + 5 for each slot level above 7th.

spells.name: Cinder-Tide
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — fire element)
spells.description: You sweep your arms wide, and a tide of roaring dragon-fire crashes across the battlefield, shaping itself around allies with draconic precision. Choose a point within range, then designate up to ten 10-foot cubes within 150 feet of that point — each cube must be adjacent to at least one other cube, forming a contiguous inferno. Each creature in the area must make a Dexterity saving throw, taking 7d10 fire damage on a failure or half on a success. The fire consumes plants in the area automatically, but you may choose for the flames to spare plant life by selectively cooling the edges of your cubes. The Cinder-Tide is the signature battlefield spell of House Pyre; their Archons can be identified by the precise geometric burn patterns they leave on warfields. Unlike a normal fireball, the Cinder-Tide carries the intelligence of the draconic bloodline that fuels it — it will not burn the caster's bloodline allies unless the caster wills it (though allies of different blood are not protected). The spell leaves the ground hot for 1 minute, dealing 2d4 fire damage to any creature that ends its turn prone on the affected area.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 8th level or higher, the damage increases by 1d10 for each slot level above 7th.

spells.name: Wyrm's Grip
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — any element, plus powdered scale worth 1,500 GC)
spells.description: You clench your fist and speak the binding-word of the First Wyrm, creating an immobile, invisible prison of force shaped from the concept of a dragon's closed claw. Choose a 20-foot cube or a 10-foot radius cage within range. For the duration, nothing — not physical matter, not magic, not even aetheric projection — can enter or exit the zone without your permission. Creatures inside cannot leave by nonmagical means. Creatures may attempt a Charisma saving throw to escape via teleportation or planar travel. The prison is immune to all damage and cannot be dispelled by Dispel Magic or similar effects of 6th level or lower. A Disintegrate spell destroys the prison instantly, as does the touch of a God-Shroud Armor. The Wyrm's Grip is visible only as a faint shimmer at the boundaries when viewed through a Dragon's Third Eye or similar truesight. The Inquisition uses this spell to hold high-value prisoners awaiting trial; its use against noble bloodlines without Citadel authorization constitutes an act of war between Houses.

spells.name: Blood-Shroud Mirage
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — storm or void element)
spells.description: You weave an illusion so vast and so profound that it bends the perception of an entire landscape into the shape of your will. Choose a square area up to 1 mile on each side within range. For the duration, you reshape the terrain's appearance, sound, smell, and even tactile sensation. A ruin can become a glittering citadel, a desert can bloom as a lush forest, a battlefield can become a tranquil lake — and the illusion is tangible enough that creatures can interact with its false structures, walk on illusory bridges, and feel the warmth of phantom fire. Structures created by the mirage can provide cover but cannot actually harm creatures (an illusory cliff cannot cause falling damage — a creature that steps off "falls" through the illusion and reappears prone on the real ground beneath). Creatures that physically interact with an illusory feature may make an Intelligence (Investigation) check against your spell save DC to disbelieve that specific feature; once disbelieved, the illusion becomes faint and translucent to that creature. The mirage does not mask creatures within the area and does not change the terrain's actual geography. This spell is used by nobles to hide their estates, by generals to deceive enemy armies, and by Penitents to conceal the entrances to patron sanctums.

spells.name: Void-Walk
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — void element, and a forked metal rod attuned to the destination plane)
spells.description: You trace the Crossing-Sigil — the pattern that marks the boundary where the World-Wyrm's body ends and the Void begins — and tear open a momentary rift. You and up to eight willing creatures who link hands in a circle are transported to another plane of existence. You may specify a target destination in general terms ("the Obsidian Court of the Void-Wyrm," "the highest peak of the Cinder-Sovereign's domain," "a location within 5 miles of the Infernal Duke's throne") and the spell places you in the nearest corresponding location. To reach a specific location, you need an object attuned to that plane — a scale from a dragon native to it, soil from its ground, water from its seas. The transition is violent: the aetheric pressure of void-walking causes all travelers to take 2d10 force damage (no save) unless they possess a purity of 50% or higher, in which case their bloodline insulates them. The rift closes behind you with a sound like a thunderclap. If cast on a creature unwilling to travel, it must make a Charisma saving throw; on a success, only that creature is left behind. This spell is the only means of reaching the Dragon Court's celestial halls without dying first. Lore-Keepers warn that void-walking too often stretches the soul thin — casters who have used this spell more than a dozen times report hearing whispers from the Void even when alone.

spells.name: Chromatic Wrath
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — fire or void element)
spells.description: You release a cone of raw chromatic dragonfire — the undiluted blood-fury of all five elemental wyrm-lines compressed into a single breath. Each creature in a 60-foot cone must make a Dexterity saving throw. For each target, roll a d8 to determine which draconic element strikes them: 1 = Fire (10d6 fire damage and ignites), 2 = Frost (10d6 cold damage and speed halved for 1 round), 3 = Storm (10d6 lightning damage and cannot take reactions for 1 round), 4 = Caustic (10d6 acid damage and AC reduced by 2 for 1 round), 5 = Venom (10d6 poison damage and poisoned for 1 minute), 6 = Radiant (10d6 radiant damage and blinded for 1 round), 7 = Void (10d6 necrotic damage and cannot regain hit points for 1 round), 8 = Roll twice, target struck by both (apply both damage types and effects). On a successful save, damage is halved and no secondary effects apply. The cone explodes in a kaleidoscopic blast — witnesses describe it as the most beautiful and terrifying thing they have ever seen, a rainbow made of pain. The Chromatic Wrath is the ultimate expression of draconic versatility, favored by Archons who refuse to limit themselves to a single bloodline element.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 8th level or higher, the damage for each element increases by 1d6 for each slot level above 7th.

spells.name: Scale-Mend
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — flesh element)
spells.description: You lay your hands upon a creature and speak the name of the Flesh-Wyrm, the dragon who shaped mortal bodies from river clay and taught wounds how to close. The target regains 4d8 + 15 hit points at the start of each of its turns for the first minute of the spell's duration (10 hit points per round at a minimum). For the remaining duration, the target regains 1 hit point at the start of each of its turns (up to 60 minutes total). Severed body members (fingers, hands, arms, legs, tail) are restored after 2 minutes of regeneration. If you have the severed part and hold it to the stump, the spell reattaches it instantly. The regeneration manifests as draconic scales forming over wounds, then flaking away to reveal healed flesh beneath. The process is warm and faintly luminous, leaving no scars except a pattern of fine scale-lines that fade over 1d4 weeks. Creatures observing the regeneration hear a distant, rhythmic thrumming — the heartbeat of the Flesh-Wyrm, still beating somewhere deep in the earth.

spells.name: Rebirth of the Bloodline
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — flesh element, and a pristine dragon scale of the same bloodline as the target, consumed by the spell)
spells.description: You perform the Rite of the Second Breath — the most sacred and most feared ritual in all of Solis-Grave. You touch a creature that has died within the last century (provided its soul is free and willing to return) and call it back to life. The target returns with all its hit points, its body restored to its prime condition, and any missing parts regenerated. The target's bloodline purity resets to its maximum (as if never diminished) and all diseases, poisons, curses, and nonmagical afflictions are purged. The ritual requires a dragon scale from a creature of the target's bloodline — without it, the soul cannot find its way back through the Sea of Aether. The ritual is physically agonizing for the caster, who takes 4d8 psychic damage that cannot be reduced (this is not Aether Burn — it is the cost of pulling a soul through the Veil). After the ritual, the caster suffers one level of exhaustion and their bloodline purity is temporarily halved for 24 hours as their body recovers from channeling the Flesh-Wyrm's essence directly. The Inquisition permits this spell only when authorized by a full Citadel council; unsanctioned resurrection is punishable by the caster's own execution. The Rite of the Second Breath cannot return a creature whose soul has been consumed by a God-Shroud, nor a creature that died of old age by natural causes.

spells.name: Inexorable Rise
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — storm or void element)
spells.description: You invert the binding-law that the World-Wyrm used to pin the earth to its place, and gravity within a 50-foot-radius, 100-foot-high cylinder centered on a point within range reverses direction. Each creature and unattached object in the cylinder falls upward, reaching the top of the area in a single round. Upon reaching the top, creatures and objects hang there, bobbing gently, until the spell ends. When the spell ends, everything falls back to the ground, taking normal falling damage. A creature may make a Dexterity saving throw when the spell is cast, catching onto a fixed object to avoid the fall. Creatures with a fly speed may attempt to arrest their ascent with a successful Strength (Athletics) check. The experience is disorienting but not inherently nauseating — most victims describe it as "the world deciding it was finished with you." This spell is the signature of the Storm-Wyrm bloodline's Archons, who use it to clear battlefields and to make dramatic entrances. The Inquisition has officially classified Inexorable Rise as a "weapon of aesthetic terror" after an incident in which a noble ball was disrupted by the entire dance floor ascending mid-waltz.

spells.name: Blood-Echo
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — flesh or void element, and a sample of the target's blood or tissue, consumed by the spell)
spells.description: You perform the Echoing Rite over a sample of a creature's blood, growing a duplicate of that creature from a vat of aether-infused clay and the blood of your own veins. The blood-echo is an illusory construct given quasi-physical form — it appears identical to the original, has the same statistics (except half the original's maximum hit points), and possesses all the original's memories, personality, and mannerisms up to the moment the blood sample was taken. The echo is friendly to you and obeys your spoken commands. It cannot gain levels, learn spells, or increase its purity; it is fixed at the state of the original at the time of creation. The echo knows it is a copy and behaves accordingly — most are either melancholic or aggressively devoted to proving their worth. If the echo drops to 0 hit points, it dissolves into a pool of colorless aether with a sound like a sigh. You may only maintain one blood-echo at a time; creating a second instantly destroys the first. The original creature retains no connection to the echo and cannot sense its existence. The Inquisition considers this spell an abomination — creating a conscious being solely to serve is, in their doctrine, a usurpation of the Dragon-Gods' creative authority. Possession of a blood-echo spell scroll carries a mandatory sentence of Pyre purification.

spells.name: Threshold Step
spells.components: V, M (Pristine Aether-Core — storm or void element)
spells.description: You speak the Traveling-Word — the Old Tongue syllable that dragons use to fold distance — and you and up to eight willing creatures within range are instantly transported to a destination you are familiar with on the same plane. Your familiarity with the destination determines the spell's reliability. A place you have visited often ("very familiar") carries no risk of mishap. A place you have seen casually or studied on a map ("seen casually") has a 25% chance of off-target arrival (d% to determine distance and direction). A place you have only heard described ("viewed once" or "description") has a 50-75% chance of off-target arrival or complete failure. A false destination — a place that doesn't exist — results in the caster taking 8d10 force damage as the aether rejects the impossible coordinate. The journey is instantaneous and silent; travelers experience a moment of total sensory deprivation — no sight, sound, or sensation — that lasts subjectively far longer than the objective instant. Threshold Steps are the preferred escape mechanism for noble families under siege, and the Citadel maintains a dedicated team of Ordained whose sole duty is to trace and counter hostile Threshold Steps targeting Cathedral City.

spells.name: Draconic Ascension
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — fire, storm, frost, void, or flesh element, matching your bloodline)
spells.description: You surrender to your bloodline completely. Your body transforms into a half-dragon hybrid form: draconic wings erupt from your shoulder blades, scales spread across your flesh like living armor, and your eyes become vertical slits burning with elemental light. For the duration, you gain a fly speed of 60 feet, your Armor Class increases by +2 as scales harden over your vital areas, and you gain immunity to the damage type matching your bloodline element (fire for cinder, lightning for storm, cold for frost, necrotic for void, acid for flesh). Your unarmed strikes deal 2d6 slashing damage as your hands become claws. Additionally, as an action, you can unleash a breath weapon in a 30-foot cone: each creature in the area must make a Dexterity saving throw, taking 8d8 damage of your bloodline's element on a failure or half on a success. You may use this breath weapon once when you transform and once again as an action on a subsequent turn. When the spell ends, the transformation recedes — wings fold back into your spine, scales sink beneath your skin — and you gain one level of exhaustion as your body reels from the strain of partial apotheosis. Draconic Ascension is the signature spell of the Sovereign class; it is the moment a Dragon-Heir proves their blood-right to all who witness. The Inquisition has no legal jurisdiction to interrupt a Draconic Ascension in progress — the act of transformation is, by Citadel law, a sacred event.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 8th level or higher, the breath weapon damage increases by 1d8 for each slot level above 7th, and at 9th level you may use the breath weapon three times instead of twice.

spells.name: Pact of the Sundered Scale
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — void or your patron's matching element, and a token of your pact)
spells.description: You tear open the aether at the site of your pact-brand, and for one terrible moment, a fragment of your patron manifests in the world — a claw, an eye, a maw of impossible geometry. Each creature of your choice within 60 feet must make a Charisma saving throw. On a failure, a creature takes 10d8 damage (the type depends on your patron: fire for the Cinder-Wyrm, lightning for the Storm-Wyrm, cold for the Frost-Wyrm, necrotic for the Void-Wyrm, acid for the Flesh-Wyrm, or radiant for a Celestial patron) and is frightened of you for 1 minute. On a success, it takes half damage and is not frightened. A creature frightened by this spell may repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on a success. The manifestation lasts only seconds, but its aftermath lingers: the ground within the radius is scarred with a pattern of draconic runes spelling the terms of your pact in the Old Tongue, and the area becomes a temporary aetheric dead zone — no spell of 3rd level or lower can be cast within the radius for 1 minute. Casting this spell deepens your pact-debt; your patron will demand a task within 1 week, and failure to comply causes your Pact Magic slots to diminish by one level (7th becomes 6th, etc.) until the debt is satisfied. This is the ultimate expression of Penitent power — and the ultimate reminder that borrowed power must be repaid.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 8th level or higher, the damage increases by 1d8 for each slot level above 7th.

spells.name: Aether Reversal
spells.casting_time: 1 reaction, which you take when you suffer Aether Burn from a failed Spell Safety save
spells.components: S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — any element)
spells.description: In the moment of Aether Burn — when the searing psychic backlash surges through your veins and your skin begins to crack with luminous pain — you slam your bloodline sigil against the current and redirect it. Instead of taking the full Aether Burn damage, you take only half. The other half is channeled outward in a 30-foot cone of your choice; each creature in the cone must make a Dexterity saving throw, taking psychic damage equal to the redirected half on a failure or no damage on a success. The backlash you redirect is raw and unstable — it appears as a spray of multicolored light, fragments of your own erased spell, and the echoing syllables of the Old Tongue you failed to speak correctly. The redirected damage carries the same properties as Aether Burn: it cannot be reduced by psychic resistance and leaves temporary glowing vein-cracks on any creature it harms. This spell can only be cast as a reaction to your own failed Spell Safety save; you cannot use it to redirect burn suffered by allies or by environmental effects. Archons who master this spell become notoriously dangerous to fight, as every miscast becomes an opportunity to harm their enemies rather than only themselves.

spells.name: Ancestral Retribution
spells.components: V, S, M (Pristine Aether-Core — fire or void element)
spells.description: You call upon the dragon-ancestors of your bloodline, naming them one by one in the Old Tongue, and a column of blazing ancestral fire descends upon a creature you can see. The target must make a Dexterity saving throw. On a failure, it takes 12d8 radiant damage plus an additional 2d8 damage for every 25% purity the target possesses (0% = +0, 25% = +2d8, 50% = +4d8, 75% = +6d8, 100% = +8d8), as the target's own bloodline becomes fuel for the fire. On a success, the target takes half damage and the purity-based bonus is halved (rounded down). The column of flame is not fire — it is the condensed judgment of dragon ancestors, white-gold in color, and it carries the sound of roaring from a dozen draconic throats. Creatures with 0% purity (Blanks) take only the base 12d8 damage; the spell considers them beneath its full wrath. The ground where the column strikes is permanently scorched with the caster's bloodline sigil, a mark that persists until magically erased. In noble blood feuds, casting Ancestral Retribution on a rival Sovereign is the ultimate declaration: I do not merely oppose you — I deny your bloodline's right to exist.
spells.higher_level: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 8th level or higher, the base damage increases by 1d8 for each slot level above 7th.
